title: What Do I Do With Regret?

situation.content:
You said the wrong thing three years ago. You took the wrong job. You didn't call when you should have. The moment is long gone — irreversible, unreachable — and yet it lives in your chest like it happened this morning.

You know you can't change it. You know dwelling helps nothing. And yet. The replay starts again. What if. If only. Why didn't I.

story.content:
Two monks — one old, one young — were walking along a muddy road after heavy rain. They came to a river crossing where a young woman stood stranded, unable to cross without ruining her silk robes.

The older monk, Tanzan, wordlessly lifted her onto his shoulders, carried her across the stream, set her down on the other bank, and continued walking. The woman thanked him and went her way.

The younger monk, Ekido, was stunned. Their monastic order strictly forbade physical contact with women. He said nothing, but his mind churned. For hours they walked in silence — through villages, past rice paddies, along the winding road to their monastery.

Finally, as evening fell, Ekido couldn't contain it any longer. "How could you do that?" he burst out. "We are monks. We are not supposed to touch women. You carried her on your shoulders!"

Tanzan looked at him calmly. "I set her down at the river," he said. "You are still carrying her."

This is the entire architecture of regret in one exchange. The event ended at the river. The carrying continued for hours — not in reality, but in the mind that refused to set it down.

What makes this story cut deeper than a simple lesson about "letting go" is what it reveals about why Ekido carried it. He wasn't carrying the woman. He was carrying his judgment of Tanzan — and underneath that, his own anxiety about rules, about what it means to be a good monk, about whether he himself would have had the courage to act compassionately in the moment. The regret wasn't about Tanzan's action. It was about Ekido's identity.

Regret always works this way. We think we're replaying the event. But we're actually replaying the story about who we are — the version that should have known better, acted faster, been braver. The event is finished. The identity threat is ongoing.

Tanzan could set the woman down because for him, the action was complete in itself — it didn't threaten his sense of who he was. Ekido couldn't set it down because it challenged his entire framework of what a monk should be. The weight wasn't the memory. It was the identity crisis the memory triggered.
story.title: The Story

contrast.content: Edith Piaf famously sang "Non, je ne regrette rien" — I regret nothing. Behind the song was a life of extraordinary loss: her daughter died at two, multiple lovers died in accidents, she battled addiction and poverty. Her refusal of regret wasn't denial — she acknowledged every loss. It was metabolization: treating past pain as the material her life was built from rather than a weight she was still carrying. The difference between Piaf and Ekido isn't the severity of the event. It's whether the past is treated as building material or as an open wound.
contrast.title: The Contrast

principle.content:
Regret isn't about the past — it's about an identity crisis in the present. You're not replaying the event. You're replaying the gap between who you were and who you think you should have been.

The event ended. The carrying is optional. Not easy to stop — but optional. You set it down not by forgetting, but by accepting that the person who made that choice was doing their best with what they had. The past self doesn't need your prosecution. It needs your compassion.

psychology.content:
Neuroscience shows that regret activates the medial orbitofrontal cortex — the same region involved in computing what we *should* have received versus what we got. It's literally a counterfactual engine: the brain models the alternative timeline and compares it to reality. This is why regret feels so vivid — your brain is simulating a world that doesn't exist with the same circuitry it uses to process reality.

Research distinguishes between "action regret" (I did something wrong) and "inaction regret" (I failed to act). In the short term, we regret actions more. But over time, inaction regrets grow stronger and last longer — the roads not taken haunt us more than the wrong turns we made. This is because actions have clear endpoints (you tried, it failed, it's over), while inactions remain permanently open (you'll never know what would have happened).

The antidote isn't suppression. Studies show that self-compassion — treating your past self the way you'd treat a friend — reduces rumination more effectively than distraction or positive reframing. The shift is from prosecutor ("how could you?") to witness ("you did what you could with what you knew").
psychology.title: Why It Happens

move.content:
1. Name what you're actually carrying. It's rarely the event itself — it's the identity gap. Ask: "What does this regret say I should have been?" That's the real weight. The event is finished. The "should have been" is what's still open.

2. Talk to your past self as a friend. If someone you loved told you they'd made the same choice with the same information, would you prosecute them for years? The compassion you'd offer them is available to you too. Your past self didn't have your current knowledge. That's not a moral failure.

3. Close the open loops where possible. Some regrets persist because they're actionable — you can still call, apologize, try again. If the regret points to something you can still do, do it. Many regrets dissolve once you take the action that's been waiting.

4. Set a deadline for carrying. Ask: "How long is reasonable to carry this?" A week? A month? Giving the grief a container prevents it from becoming permanent background noise. Tanzan set her down immediately. You might need longer. But a timeline helps.

5. Convert regret to policy, not punishment. "I should have spoken up" becomes "I now speak up in the first meeting." Regret that updates your future behavior has done its job and can be released. Regret that only punishes your past self is just pain without purpose.
move.title: The Move
